modules.title: First Output
modules.description: Print text to the console with println!, print!, and dbg!

modules.title: Variables & Bindings
modules.description: Declare variables with let, use mut, type annotations, and shadowing

modules.title: Functions
modules.description: Define functions with params, return types, and implicit returns

modules.title: Control Flow
modules.description: if/else, loop, for, while, and match expressions

modules.title: Ownership Basics
modules.description: String, move semantics, clone, and borrowing

modules.title: Structs
modules.description: Define structs, instantiate them, access fields, and impl methods

challenges.title: Hello, Rust!
challenges.prompt: Print the text Hello, Rust! to the console
challenges.hint_concept: In Rust, println! is a macro that prints text followed by a newline. Note the ! — that marks it as a macro, not a regular function.
challenges.explanation: println! is a macro — note the !. Macros in Rust are expanded at compile time. The parentheses wrap the arguments, and the semicolon ends the statement.

challenges.title: Format a variable
challenges.prompt: Print the value of x to the console using a format placeholder
challenges.hint_concept: Use {} inside a string as a placeholder. After the string, add a comma and the variable whose value fills the placeholder.
challenges.explanation: The {} placeholder in the format string gets replaced by the value of x at runtime. You can have multiple {} placeholders with multiple comma-separated values.

challenges.title: Two format arguments
challenges.prompt: Print the values of both a and b on the same line, separated by a space
challenges.hint_concept: You can use multiple {} placeholders in one format string. Each one is filled in order by the comma-separated values that follow.
challenges.explanation: Each {} is filled in order: the first {} gets a, the second {} gets b. Rust checks at compile time that the number of placeholders matches the number of arguments.

challenges.title: Print without newline
challenges.prompt: Output the text Hello to the console without adding a newline at the end
challenges.hint_concept: There's a variant of the print macro that doesn't add a newline. It works the same way, just with a slightly different name.
challenges.explanation: print! is the no-newline version of println!. The output stays on the same line, which is useful for building up output incrementally.

challenges.title: Debug a value
challenges.prompt: Use the debug macro to inspect the current value of x
challenges.hint_concept: There's a macro designed for quick debugging that prints the expression, its value, and the file/line — all to stderr.
challenges.explanation: dbg! outputs to stderr with file:line info — perfect for debugging. Unlike println!, it returns the value it wraps, so you can use it inline.

challenges.title: Declare a variable
challenges.prompt: Create an immutable variable called x that holds the number 42
challenges.hint_concept: Variables in Rust are immutable by default. You declare them with a keyword followed by the name, an equals sign, and the value.
challenges.explanation: let creates a new variable binding. Variables in Rust are immutable by default — you can't reassign x after this unless you use mut.

challenges.title: Mutable variable
challenges.prompt: Declare a variable x that can be changed later, starting at 0
challenges.hint_concept: To make a variable reassignable, you need an extra keyword between the declaration keyword and the variable name.
challenges.explanation: mut marks a variable as mutable. Rust makes you opt into mutability explicitly — this helps catch accidental mutations.

challenges.title: Reassign a value
challenges.prompt: Change the value of x to 10 (assume x was declared as mutable)
challenges.hint_concept: Reassigning doesn't need the declaration keyword again — just the variable name, equals, and the new value.
challenges.explanation: Reassignment uses = without let. This only works if x was declared with let mut. Trying to reassign an immutable variable causes a compile error.

challenges.title: Type annotation
challenges.prompt: Declare a variable x and explicitly specify that its type is a 32-bit integer, with value 5
challenges.hint_concept: You can tell the compiler the exact type of a variable by placing a colon and the type name between the variable name and the equals sign.
challenges.explanation: The : i32 is a type annotation. Rust can usually infer types, but explicit annotations are useful when the type isn't obvious or you want to be specific.

challenges.title: Shadow a variable
challenges.prompt: Declare a brand new x with value 100, shadowing any previous x in this scope
challenges.hint_concept: You can declare a new variable with the same name as an existing one. The new declaration shadows the old one in the current scope.
challenges.explanation: Shadowing creates a completely new variable that happens to share the name. Unlike mut, shadowing lets you change the type too.

challenges.title: Empty function
challenges.prompt: Define the program's entry point function — it takes no arguments and returns nothing
challenges.hint_concept: Every Rust program starts from a specific function. You declare functions with a keyword, then the name, parentheses for parameters, and curly braces for the body.
challenges.explanation: fn declares a function. main is special — it's the entry point of every Rust program. The empty () means no parameters, and {} is the empty body.
